questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A heterotherm is an organism that can regulate its body temperature, but not to a constant level. Instead, it may exhibit fluctuations in body temperature based on environmental conditions. This is in contrast to homeotherms, which maintain a relatively constant internal temperature. Heterothermy allows organisms to conserve energy by adjusting their metabolic rate in response to temperature variations.

A poikilotherm is an organism whose body temperature varies with and is largely dependent on the ambient environment. Poikilotherms, also known as ectotherms, cannot internally regulate their temperature and rely on external sources, such as sunlight or the surrounding air, to influence their metabolic activity. Most reptiles, amphibians, and many fish are examples of poikilothermic animals.

An ectotherm is an organism that regulates its body temperature primarily through external sources, such as the environment. Ectothermic animals, also known as poikilotherms, cannot internally generate heat to maintain a constant body temperature. Instead, their metabolic processes and activity levels are influenced by external conditions, such as sunlight or ambient air temperature.

An endotherm is an organism that can internally regulate and maintain a relatively constant body temperature, independent of the external environment. Endothermic animals, also known as homeotherms or warm-blooded organisms, achieve temperature regulation through metabolic processes that generate heat.

Similar to it in living system, there is set point in temperature regulated (endothermic) animals. The receptors (sensor) detect temperature change, e.g., of increase and signal to control center for action of cooling systems and the vice versa.
